subject: [PATCH v2] mm/slub: serve slabobj_ext array from a strictly larger kmalloc cache

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo noted that the patch makes it easier to trigger a pre-existing issue where freeing a slab in an unknown context can leak the obj_exts array due to kfree_nolock() not supporting large kmalloc, but he believes this scenario is unreachable because trylock fails before allocating a new slab.
reviews.raw_body:
Looks good to me so:
Reviewed-by: Harry Yoo (Oracle) <harry@kernel.org>

and it also passed my test suite, so:
Tested-by: Harry Yoo (Oracle) <harry@kernel.org>

Interestingly, Sashiko pointed out one issue [1] that
doesn't sound completely wrong. But that's a pre-existing one
and although Sashiko (presumably) thinks this patch makes it easier
to trigger this, I think the scenario is unreachable.

[1]
https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630024357.3591304-1-shakeel.butt%40linux.dev

Here's why I don't think anybody would be hitting it:

It says if s->object_size == KMALLOC_MAX_CACHE_SIZE,
alloc_slab_obj_exts() will always fail with SLAB_ALLOC_NOLOCK because
kmalloc_nolock() does not support large kmalloc.

Then a later allocation of slab objects allocates obj_exts array
(with large kmalloc), and freeing of the slab in unknown context tries
to free the obj_exts array, which kfree_nolock() doesn't support and
leaks the obj_exts array.

However, freeing slab in unknown context is done only when trylock
fails after allocating a new slab. So it's unreachable.

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

2026-06-30.patch_summary: This patch fixes a kernel stack overflow bug caused by a cycle of recursive slab object frees. The issue occurs when the obj_exts array, used for allocation profiling and memcg accounting, is allocated from a kmalloc cache that is the same size as or smaller than the one it describes. To fix this, the patch serves the array from a strictly larger kmalloc cache to break the cycle and prevent the recursion. This approach ensures that every reference edge points from a smaller to a larger cache, making the relation a directed acyclic graph (DAG) and preventing cycles.
